title: Collections
summary: Choose the right collection type: lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries for different use cases

sections.title: Different tools for different jobs
sections.content: If you only had a hammer, every problem would look like a nail. Python has four built-in collection types, each designed for different scenarios. Lists are ordered, mutable, and allow duplicates — great for sequences like a playlist. Tuples are ordered and immutable — use them for fixed data like coordinates or configuration. Sets are unordered, mutable, but contain only unique items — perfect for eliminating duplicates or membership tests. Dictionaries store key-value pairs — like a real dictionary mapping a word to its definition. Choosing the right one makes your code clearer and more performant.

sections.title: Using all four collection types
sections.content: This example demonstrates the right use case for each collection
sections.code:
# Set — unique items only, use for deduplication and membership tests
unique_visitors = {"Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Alice"}  # Alice appears once
print("Unique visitors:", unique_visitors)
print("Is Bob here?", "Bob" in unique_visitors)  # Fast membership check

# Tuple — immutable, use for fixed data that shouldn't change
coordinates = (40.7128, -74.0060)  # Latitude and longitude of NYC
# coordinates[0] = 50  # Would raise TypeError — tuples are immutable
print("Latitude:", coordinates[0])  # Access by index like a list

# List — ordered, mutable, use for sequences that change
shopping_cart = ["Apples", "Milk", "Bread"]  # Items can be added/removed
shopping_cart.append("Eggs")  # Add an item — lists are mutable
print("Cart:", shopping_cart)

# Dictionary — key-value mapping, use for lookup tables
prices = {"Apples": 0.50, "Milk": 2.99, "Bread": 3.49}
print("Price of milk:", prices["Milk"])  # Fast lookup by key
# Expected output:
# Unique visitors: {'Charlie', 'Alice', 'Bob'}
# Is Bob here? True
# Latitude: 40.7128
# Cart: ['Apples', 'Milk', 'Bread', 'Eggs']
# Price of milk: 2.99

sections.title: When to use each collection — trade-offs
sections.content: Choose tuples for data that represents a single thing with multiple parts (like a point in 3D space: (x, y, z)) — they're hashable (usable as dictionary keys) and signal 'this won't change.' Choose sets when you need fast 'is this in the collection?' checks — O(1) average time vs O(n) for lists — and when duplicates don't make sense. Choose dictionaries for any mapping relationship: user IDs to profiles, product codes to prices. Choose lists for ordered sequences where you need indexing, slicing, and duplicates. Using lists everywhere because they're familiar is a missed opportunity for clearer, faster code.

sections.title: Unintended dictionary key mutations
sections.content: Beginners at intermediate level still accidentally use mutable objects as dictionary keys. Wrong: my_dict = {[1, 2]: 'value'} — lists are unhashable and raise TypeError. Correct: use tuples instead: my_dict = {(1, 2): 'value'}. Only immutable types (strings, numbers, tuples) can be dictionary keys or set members.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A conference tracks attendees. Store 5 attendee names in a list (ordered registration). Use a set to find out how many unique companies they represent (company names). Use a dictionary to map each attendee name to their company. Print the unique company count and the dictionary.
hints: Which collection type automatically removes duplicates?
How do you create a dictionary where each name maps to a company?
